title: CPU Sizing Guide
summary: Understand workload type, worker concurrency, per-worker CPU pressure, burst behavior, utilization target, and SMT assumptions before selecting compute resources.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether the CPU allocation has enough practical core capacity and operating margin for the workload profile you enter.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when sizing servers, virtual machines, application hosts, analytics nodes, batch workers, or any system where processor headroom affects response time and stability.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible CPU Sizing Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects compute pressure.

items.label: Workload Type
items.description: The broad workload profile the CPU will support. The tool uses this to adjust how aggressively the entered worker load should be treated.
items.whyItMatters: Different workloads consume CPU differently. A light web service, database, analytics process, and VM host can all behave very differently at the same apparent worker count.
items.commonMistake: Using one generic CPU rule for every workload type.

links.label: Compare memory behavior
links.note: Memory planning should use the same workload story, not a separate assumption.

items.label: Concurrent Workers / Threads
items.description: The number of workers, threads, tasks, users, or sessions that may be active at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Concurrency turns a small per-worker load into a larger system-level CPU requirement.
items.commonMistake: Sizing around one worker or an average user while ignoring busy-period concurrency.

items.label: Avg CPU per Worker (%)
items.description: The estimated average CPU demand created by each active worker or thread.
items.whyItMatters: This value is multiplied by concurrency, so a small error can become large when many workers are active.
items.commonMistake: Entering a best-case lab number instead of a practical sustained estimate.

items.label: Peak Factor (burst)
items.description: A multiplier for busy periods, spikes, reporting jobs, shift changes, or other non-average behavior.
items.whyItMatters: Peak factor helps avoid designing only for calm steady-state operation.
items.commonMistake: Leaving peak factor near 1.0 when the workload has known bursts.

items.label: Target Max CPU Utilization (%)
items.description: The maximum steady-state utilization you want the design to stay under.
items.whyItMatters: Lower targets leave more room for bursts, background tasks, and future growth.
items.commonMistake: Calling a CPU design efficient because it runs close to 100% all the time.

links.label: See density impact
links.note: Tight CPU targets become more important when many VMs share the same host.

items.label: Hyperthreading / SMT
items.description: Whether logical threads are being treated as available execution capacity.
items.whyItMatters: SMT can improve throughput, but logical threads are not the same as full physical cores under every workload.
items.commonMistake: Counting logical threads as if each one were an independent physical core.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the CPU plan has reasonable operating margin. Watch means the workload may still run, but burst behavior, thread count, or target utilization should be reviewed. Risk means the design may queue, slow down, or require more CPU capacity before it is reliable.
bullets: Recommended cores are a planning estimate, not a hardware guarantee.
Dominant constraint points to the first pressure area to review.
A healthy score still depends on realistic concurrency and burst assumptions.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: CPU Sizing is the first Compute flow step because it sets the processor baseline that later RAM, storage, density, GPU, power, rebuild, and backup decisions build on.
bullets: Start here when the compute workload shape is still unknown.
Use the same workload story in RAM Sizing next.
Do not treat CPU sizing as final until memory and storage pressure are checked.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
